metadata.summary: Weighted decision matrix construction with criteria, scoring, sensitivity analysis, and standard 2-4h trade-off checklist for architecture option comparison.

content.title: Trade-off Decision Matrix
content.summary: Build a weighted decision matrix to compare 3-5 architecture options across scored criteria. Run sensitivity analysis to verify the winner is stable. The standard 2-4h checklist covers context, option discovery, criteria definition, option evaluation, trade-off identification, risk analysis, and documentation.

why.p: Decision matrices make implicit criteria explicit and force consistent scoring. Without a matrix, teams unconsciously weight criteria differently in their heads and cannot surface disagreements. The weighted score exposes which criteria dominate the decision and lets teams ask "what if we cared more about cost?" before committing. Sensitivity analysis prevents false confidence when the top two options are within 5% of each other.

list.item: Technology selection or vendor comparison with 3-5 viable options.
Any decision where criteria weights are contested among stakeholders.
Standard 2-4h trade-off analysis for significant technical decisions with moderate impact.
When you need to document the objective basis for a recommendation to stakeholders.
Type-1 irreversible decisions with major business impact — use ATAM for deeper analysis instead of just a matrix.
Type-2 reversible decisions where the wrong choice costs less than the analysis — decide fast and iterate.
When criteria cannot be agreed on first — resolve stakeholder alignment before scoring.

rule.statement: Use a 1-5 scoring scale (not 1-10) for both weights and scores. Calculate weighted score = raw score x weight. Sum weighted scores per option. Run sensitivity analysis: identify which criteria most influence the outcome and test what happens if the top criterion weight changes by ±2.
rule.rationale: Humans cannot reliably distinguish 7 from 8 on a 1-10 scale, and LLMs bias high. 1-5 gives enough granularity while keeping scores meaningful. Sensitivity analysis prevents false confidence in a matrix where top options are within 5% — if they tie within 5%, the criteria are too coarse.

example.title: Decision matrix — database selection for e-commerce
example.code:
Decision: Database for product catalog (50K daily users, 10M products, 1M daily searches)
Options: PostgreSQL | MongoDB | Elasticsearch+PostgreSQL | CockroachDB
Criteria + Weights: - Query performance: 5 - Team expertise: 4 - Schema flexibility: 3 - Operational cost: 4 - Future scalability: 3 - Migration effort: 3
Scoring (raw score × weight): | Criteria | W | PostgreSQL | MongoDB | ES+PG | CockroachDB | |----------------|---|------------|---------|-------|-------------| | Query perf | 5 | 4 (20) | 3 (15) | 5(25) | 4 (20) | | Team expertise | 4 | 5 (20) | 2 (8) | 3(12) | 3 (12) | | Schema flex | 3 | 3 (9) | 5 (15) | 4(12) | 3 (9) | | Ops cost | 4 | 5 (20) | 3 (12) | 2 (8) | 3 (12) | | Scalability | 3 | 3 (9) | 4 (12) | 4(12) | 5 (15) | | Migration | 3 | 5 (15) | 2 (6) | 2 (6) | 4 (12) | | Total | | 93 | 68 | 75 | 80 |
Sensitivity: if query performance weight rises to 5→5 and migration weight drops to 3→1, ES+PG overtakes PostgreSQL. Warrants discussion before committing.
example.note: Despite PostgreSQL scoring highest, the team chose ES+PG for search requirements — documenting the explicit override of the matrix score in the ADR.

antipattern.description: Accepting the matrix winner without checking if top-2 scores are within 5% of each other.
antipattern.reason: A margin of 3-5% on a weighted matrix often means the criteria weights are wrong, not that one option is clearly better. Flag ties and revisit weight rationale with stakeholders.

section.name: standard-checklist
section.p: Standard Trade-off Analysis Checklist (2-4 hours) for significant technical decisions with moderate impact.

how-to.step: Phase 1 — Context Establishment (20 min): document the problem statement (what problem are we solving, what triggered this decision, what happens if we do not decide), identify stakeholders (affected parties, input authority, decision authority), gather constraints (budget, timeline, technical, regulatory/compliance, team capability), define success criteria (how will we know this was a good decision, what metrics matter).

Phase 2 — Option Discovery (30 min): brainstorm options (include obvious solutions, "do nothing", contrarian options, hybrid approaches), research each option (industry adoption, vendor stability if applicable, community/ecosystem health, integration with existing systems), perform initial feasibility check (technical, resource, timeline), shortlist to 3-5 options.

Phase 3 — Criteria Definition (20 min): list quality attributes relevant to the decision (performance: latency/throughput; scalability: horizontal/vertical; availability: uptime/recovery; security: confidentiality/integrity; maintainability: modifiability/testability; cost: initial/operational/total), add business criteria (time to market, team expertise/learning curve, vendor relationship, strategic alignment), assign weights (1-5 scale, validate with stakeholders, document rationale for weights).

Phase 4 — Option Evaluation (45 min): score each option against each criterion (1-5 consistent scale, document scoring rationale, flag uncertain scores for discussion), calculate weighted scores (weighted score = raw score x weight, sum per option), identify sensitivity points (which criteria most influence outcome, what if weights changed), create decision matrix.

Phase 5 — Trade-off Identification (30 min): for each option document what we gain (benefits), what we lose (sacrifices), what risks we accept, what risks we avoid; identify trade-off points (where does improving one criterion hurt another, which trade-offs are acceptable, which are deal-breakers); consider second-order effects (impact on adjacent systems, team dynamics, future decisions).

Phase 6 — Risk Analysis (20 min): for recommended option identify technical, operational, business, integration risks; assess each risk (probability H/M/L, impact H/M/L, risk score = probability x impact); define mitigations (strategy for high risks, contingency plans, risk monitoring approach).

Phase 7 — Decision and Documentation (15 min): state recommended option, summarize key trade-offs accepted, list conditions/assumptions; document in ADR (context and problem, options considered, decision and rationale, trade-offs accepted, consequences); plan communication (who needs to know, what format, when).

section.name: quick-decision-checklist
section.p: Quick Decision Checklist (30 minutes) for Type 2 reversible decisions and time-sensitive choices.

checklist.check: Define the decision to be made in one sentence.
Classify decision type: Type 1 irreversible or Type 2 reversible.
List at least 3 viable options (including "do nothing").
Identify top 3 criteria for this decision.
Identify biggest risk for each option.
Check: What do we lose with each option?
Select option with best fit to criteria.

section.name: llm-prompts
section.prompt:
Create a weighted decision matrix for choosing between: - Option A: [description] - Option B: [description] - Option C: [description]
Context: [business and technical context] Constraints: [list constraints]
Business priorities (rank 1-5, 5 being highest): - Time to market: [priority] - Cost: [priority] - Scalability: [priority] - Maintainability: [priority] - [Custom priority]: [priority]
Generate: 1. Evaluation criteria with recommended weights (justify weights) 2. Score each option (1-5) with brief rationale 3. Calculate weighted totals 4. Sensitivity analysis: what changes would flip the decision? 5. Final recommendation with confidence level

Analyze trade-offs between [Option A] and [Option B] for [context/use case].
Consider these quality attributes: Performance, Scalability, Maintainability, Security, Cost (initial and operational), Team expertise required.
Format as: ## Option A: [Name] **What you gain:** [bullet list] **What you sacrifice:** [bullet list] **Best when:** [scenarios where A is clearly better]
## Option B: [Name] [Same format]
## Trade-off Matrix | Attribute | Option A | Option B | Winner |
## Recommendation Based on [context], recommend [option] because [reasons].

I'm leaning toward [Option X] for [decision]. Arguments for X: [my reasons]
Now argue AGAINST this decision. Be a rigorous devil's advocate: 1. What are the strongest arguments for alternatives? 2. What am I not seeing or underweighting? 3. What could make this decision catastrophically wrong? 4. What assumptions am I making that might not hold? 5. Who would disagree with this and why might they be right?
Don't hold back — help me stress-test this decision.

Help me conduct a post-implementation review of this trade-off decision:
Original decision (date): [What we decided] Predicted trade-offs: [What we expected] Predicted risks: [What we anticipated]
Actual outcomes: - [Outcome 1] - [Outcome 2]
Analyze: 1. Did our predicted trade-offs materialize as expected? 2. Were there unexpected trade-offs? 3. Did our risk mitigations work? 4. What would we do differently? 5. What lessons should we document?
Format as a retrospective with actionable learnings.

list.item: Force the agent to declare reversibility (Type 1 vs Type 2) up front; analysis depth follows.
Demand one concrete failure scenario per winning option; absent that, the analysis is theatre.
Capture the criteria you considered and rejected — future readers learn as much from those.
Tag every ADR with the quality attributes it touches so the architect skill can cross-reference.
Run the same matrix for the runner-up option as a sanity check; if they tie within 5%, the criteria are too coarse.
LLMs treat "cost" as a single criterion; split into capex, opex, opportunity cost, and switching cost.
"What do we lose?" is the magic question — without it agents only enumerate gains.

antipattern.description: Letting one agent both generate options and score them.
antipattern.reason: An agent scores its own ideas higher (selection bias). Use separate subagent roles for option generation, scoring, and red-teaming.

antipattern.description: Cargo-culting: citing "FAANG uses X" without analyzing trade-offs in your context.
antipattern.reason: Your team size, budget, regulatory environment, and hiring market may be entirely different. Always ask for trade-offs in your context, not theirs.
